title: Map coarse location to palette
summary: Replace the current hash-only geo-to-palette mapping with a structured palette-family system that maps coarse geographic classes (continental/hemispheric regions) to perceptible colour families, while keeping the session seed for within-family variation. The current geoToPalette function in mappings.ts hashes country+region+seed into an arbitrary hue — this ticket upgrades it to use intentional, partially legible palette families (e.g. warm/tropical, cool/northern, earthy/continental) so that geography subtly but perceptibly influences the scene.

existing_code.src/visual/mappings.ts: Contains geoToPalette(country, region, seed) which hashes geo+seed into a hue (0-360) and saturation (0.3-0.8). Also contains mapSignalsToVisuals() which calls geoToPalette and returns VisualParams including paletteHue and paletteSaturation.
existing_code.src/visual/evolution.ts: Time-based harmonic drift for paletteHue (±15-30°), paletteSaturation (±0.05-0.15). Already applies on top of base palette values.
existing_code.src/visual/systems/particleField.ts: Renders particles using hsla(paletteHue + perParticleOffset, paletteSaturation, lightness, alpha). Already consumes palette values from VisualParams.
existing_code.src/input/geo.ts: Exports GeoHint { country: string|null, region: string|null } and fetchGeoHint(). Already working.
existing_code.api/geo.ts: Vercel edge function returning coarse { country, region } with no-store cache. Already working.
existing_code.tests/visual/mappings.test.ts: 21 tests covering mappings including geo-to-palette consistency, range checks, null fallback, and privacy.

steps.title: Define palette family types and geo classification table
steps.description: Create a new module that defines the palette family system. Define a PaletteFamily interface with: id (string), hueCenter (number 0-360), hueRange (number, spread around center), saturationBase (number 0.3-0.8), and warmth ('warm'|'cool'|'neutral'). Define 6-8 palette families covering the colour wheel (e.g. 'tropical' hueCenter ~30 warm, 'oceanic' ~200 cool, 'boreal' ~160 cool, 'desert' ~45 warm, 'temperate' ~120 neutral, 'aurora' ~280 cool, 'volcanic' ~350 warm). Create a coarse geo classification function classifyGeo(country: string|null, region: string|null) that maps countries to continental/climatic classes using a lightweight lookup table of ISO country codes grouped by broad region (e.g. tropical belt countries, northern hemisphere, southern hemisphere, oceanic). The classification must use coarse groupings — not exact country-to-colour mapping — so the mapping is partially legible but not a geography quiz. Return a class string like 'tropical'|'northern'|'southern'|'oceanic'|'continental'|'unknown'.
steps.details: The geo classification table should group ~20-30 common country codes into 5-6 broad classes. Countries not in the table fall back to 'unknown'. The table is intentionally coarse — it groups Brazil, Indonesia, Thailand together as 'tropical' rather than distinguishing them. This is the key artistic decision: geography influences palette in a way that's perceptible but not precise.
tests: classifyGeo returns a valid class string for known countries
classifyGeo returns 'unknown' for null country
classifyGeo returns 'unknown' for unrecognized country codes
Each geo class maps to a distinct palette family
Palette families have valid hue/saturation ranges

steps.title: Upgrade geoToPalette to use palette families with seeded variation
steps.description: Replace the current geoToPalette function body. Import classifyGeo and the palette families from palette.ts. The new logic: (1) classify the geo into a coarse class via classifyGeo, (2) look up the corresponding PaletteFamily, (3) use simpleHash(class + seed) to pick a specific hue within the family's hueCenter ± hueRange, and (4) derive saturation from the family's saturationBase with seed-based variation ±0.1. For the 'unknown' class (null geo fallback), use a neutral family with moderate saturation and a seed-derived hue across the full 0-360 range — this is the fallback palette. The function signature stays the same: geoToPalette(country, region, seed) → { hue, saturation }. The output types and ranges stay the same so downstream code (evolution, particleField) needs no changes.
steps.details: The key change is that instead of hash(geo+seed) % 360 producing an arbitrary hue, the hue is now constrained to a family range. A 'tropical' visitor gets warm hues (20-60°), a 'boreal' visitor gets cool green-cyan hues (140-200°), etc. The session seed shifts the exact value within that range, so two tropical visitors on different sessions see different-but-similar warm palettes. This makes the mapping partially legible — a viewer in Tokyo and a viewer in São Paulo will see different colour families.
tests: Known tropical country produces hue in warm range (roughly 15-65°)
Known northern country produces hue in cool range (roughly 140-220°)
null/null geo produces a valid fallback palette (full hue range allowed)
Same country + same seed produces identical hue and saturation (deterministic)
Same country + different seed produces different exact hue within same family
Different geo classes produce different hue families (at least 3 distinct ranges)
Output contains no raw country codes or region strings (privacy check)
paletteSaturation stays within 0.3-0.8 for all families

steps.title: Update existing mapping tests for palette-family behaviour
steps.description: Update T-008-03 (different countries produce different hues) to verify that different geo classes produce hues in different ranges, not just different exact values. Update T-008-05 (range check) to verify hue stays within the expected family range for known countries. Add new test cases specific to US-010 acceptance criteria: (1) geo influences palette family when available — known country produces hue constrained to its family range; (2) fallback palette used when geo null — null geo produces valid palette without throwing; (3) raw geo values not in output — JSON.stringify of VisualParams contains no country/region strings; (4) deterministic within session — same inputs produce identical palette values.
steps.details: Keep the existing T-008-19 privacy test and T-008-04 consistency test as they already cover criteria 3 and 4. The new tests should form a describe block 'US-010: Map coarse location to palette' to clearly trace coverage to this ticket.

steps.title: Add unit tests for palette.ts classification and family logic
steps.description: Create dedicated tests for the palette module: test classifyGeo with representative countries from each class, null inputs, and unknown codes. Test that each geo class resolves to a palette family with valid hueCenter, hueRange, and saturationBase. Test that the family lookup is exhaustive — every class returned by classifyGeo has a corresponding family entry.
tests: classifyGeo('BR', region) returns 'tropical'
classifyGeo('NO', region) returns 'northern'
classifyGeo('AU', region) returns 'southern' or 'oceanic'
classifyGeo(null, null) returns 'unknown'
classifyGeo('XX', null) returns 'unknown'
Every geo class has a valid palette family entry
All palette family hueRanges are positive and ≤ 60
All palette family saturationBase values are in 0.3-0.8

steps.title: Verify integration — build, tests, and runtime checks
steps.description: Run npm run build to confirm no type errors. Run npm run test to confirm all tests pass (existing + new). Manually verify in dev server that: (1) scene renders with coloured particles, (2) no console errors, (3) no localStorage/cookie access in Application tab, (4) palette feels cohesive (hues within a family, not random). Check that the evolution system still drifts hue smoothly within ±15-30° of the family-derived base — this should work without changes since evolution operates on the numeric paletteHue output.
checks: npm run build passes with no errors
npm run test passes with all tests green
No console errors on page load
Scene renders with visible palette influence
Mute toggle and info overlay still functional

acceptance_criteria_mapping.Country or region influences palette fam: Step 1 classifies geo into coarse classes; Step 2 maps classes to palette families with constrained hue ranges. Verified by tests showing known countries produce hues within their family range.
acceptance_criteria_mapping.Fallback palette is used when geo hints: Step 2 handles null country/region via 'unknown' class which maps to a neutral full-range fallback. Verified by null-input tests.
acceptance_criteria_mapping.Raw geo values are not displayed in the: No changes to rendering code. geoToPalette still outputs only numeric hue/saturation. Privacy tests verify no raw strings in VisualParams. The classification table is internal and never surfaces in output.
acceptance_criteria_mapping.Palette changes are deterministic within: Session seed is mixed into hue derivation within the palette family. Same seed + same geo = same exact hue. Verified by determinism tests.

risks.risk: Geo classification table too small — many visitors fall into 'unknown'
risks.mitigation: Include the ~30 most common countries by internet traffic. The fallback palette is still functional and visually valid — it just spans the full hue range instead of a family.

risks.risk: Palette families too narrow — evolution drift escapes the family range
risks.mitigation: Evolution already drifts ±15-30° from base. Palette families should have hueRange ≥ 25 so drift stays mostly within family. Some drift outside is acceptable and adds organic variety.

risks.risk: Existing test T-008-19 breaks because 'US' or 'CA' might appear as a substring in numeric output
risks.mitigation: This test checks JSON.stringify of the output object. Since output is all numbers, two-letter strings won't appear. But verify the test still passes — the hue number could theoretically contain digits that spell something, but JSON numbers don't have string substrings of 'US' or 'CA'.
